text: Hello. I am sharing a low anterior for a rectal cancer after TNT. This is a patient with a left pelvic kidney. That's what you were looking at there. She's a very high BMI. The Keith needle wouldn't even pass through the abdominal wall, so I had to suture it intercorporeal. And the rectus sigmoid lateral attachments were actually on the right side. I'm not sure congenitally if that has anything to do with the pelvic kidney or just something strange, but it did provide a little bit of a challenge as I had to mobilize the sigmoid the opposite direction.

The surgeon navigates the mobilization and total mesorectal excision (TME) dissection, with the pelvic kidney visible on the left displacing the rectosigmoid mesentery to the right. The surgeon follows the right side of the TME plane, correcting course upon getting lost in the sigmoid mesentery, and identifies the proper retrorectal space over the promontory. The superior hemorrhoidal vessel is visible, displaced to the patient's right.

The surgeon carefully dissects along the superior hemorrhoidal vessel, aiming to reach the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) while separating unusual right-sided lateral attachments of the sigmoid colon. This dissection is necessary to achieve sufficient length for the planned coloanal anastomosis.
The surgeon attempts to dissect underneath the superior hemorrhoidal vessel to facilitate its removal, preparing to continue with the total mesorectal excision (TME) dissection.
I do think that... ICG was helpful in this case to show me how far lateral the left ureter was. And so using it on and off really helped me to stay out of trouble.

The surgeon outlines the planned total mesorectal excision (TME) dissection, focusing on the anterior aspect within the rectovaginal septum. The pelvic kidney is visible, with the ureter positioned laterally. The surgeon attempts to connect the anatomical structures beneath the sigmoid mesentery, creating a window over the kidney.

The surgeon focuses on the superior hemorrhoidal IMA pedicle, noting its position to the patient's right during dissection. A vessel sealer is used due to increased vascularity around the mesentery. The ureter is visible extending laterally, and the surgeon aims to create a window around the pedicle.

The surgeon switches to scissors for finer dissection, targeting the vessel on the right identified as the superior hemorrhoidal. The surgeon takes this vessel and identifies a branch as the left colic, which is also taken distally to achieve necessary length.
The surgeon identifies the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV) and completes its dissection, preparing to proceed with the total mesorectal excision (TME) dissection.

The surgeon navigates the left side of the pelvis, avoiding the left-sided pelvic kidney while performing a circumferential dissection. The camera is transitioned to arm number two and port hopped to the right for a direct view of the deeper pelvis. The wide pelvis facilitates a standard dissection process, following neoadjuvant radiation completed ten weeks prior.

The surgeon performs anterior dissection, carefully avoiding the vagina. The tumor is located approximately six centimeters from the anal verge, with a black tattoo marking just distal to the tumor at the level of the anorectal ring.

The surgeon performs a total mesorectal excision with colloanal anastomosis, using monopolar cautery to dissect the mesorectum from the floor of the pelvis. The camera is positioned in the epigastrium midline, providing a straight view into the deep pelvis. The levators are visible on the patient's right side as the surgeon carefully peels the mesorectum away.

The surgeon performs dissection on the left side, mirroring the plane previously established on the right. The levators are visible on the patient's left side. The surgeon flips posteriorly for a better view, lifting the mesorectum off the pelvic levator floor. The tattoo marking is visible near the surgeon's left hand grasper. The surgeon prepares for stapling by pointing at the rectal wall, completing the total mesorectal excision.

The surgeon carefully avoids the vagina while completing the dissection. The proximal mesentery is taken, and the specimen is extracted through the upsized 12mm trocar in arm number three, avoiding a separate extraction incision in the higher BMI patient.

The surgeon takes the mesentery intracorporeally to prevent tearing during extraction. The anvil is placed in a baker orientation, and the stapled rectum is visible withdrawn into the levator floor, indicating a low stapled anastomosis. The colon is brought in, and the surgeon staples the anastomosis while the robot holds the anvil. A leak test is performed, which is negative, and a diverting loop ileostomy is created. The final pathology shows a T2 N0 post-treatment tumor.
